commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=2fcbb08866b377a5998e69c5bd8dc6c28d443eeb)
commands.review_summary:
The PR changes three browser snapshot text-label truncations from raw `.slice(0, N)` to string-spread code-point slicing to avoid splitting surrogate pairs.

PR surface: Source +4. Total +4 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: yes. from source inspection: current main uses raw `.slice(0, 100)` and `.slice(0, 120)` on browser snapshot labels, so an astral character at the cut boundary can leave a dangling surrogate half. I did not run a live browser snapshot reproduction in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] The contributor needs to address the lint finding and provide real behavior proof; ClawSweeper should not repair-marker this external PR while the proof gate requires contributor evidence.

target.title: fix(browser): truncate element text on code-point boundaries to avoid lone surrogates
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Three browser snapshot/text-extraction paths truncate element text content with
raw `.slice(0, N)`. When an emoji or other astral character straddles the cut
point, the raw slice keeps only the high-surrogate half, producing a lone
`\uD83D` in the LLM context — malformed UTF-16.

Affected paths:
- `cdp.ts`: `findCursorInteractiveElements` evaluates page script that truncates
  `textContent` at 100 code units
- `pw-tools-core.snapshot.ts`: `collectSnapshotUrls` truncates anchor label at
  120 code units
- `routes/agent.snapshot.ts`: `collectChromeMcpSnapshotUrls` truncates anchor
  label at 120 code units

## Why This Change Was Made

Since the affected code runs in the browser page context (via CDP `Runtime.evaluate`
and Playwright `page.evaluate`), the SDK's `truncateUtf16Safe` is not importable.
Instead, the spread operator `[...str]` splits the string by Unicode code points,
and `.slice(0, N).join("")` takes the first N code points — ensuring surrogate
pairs are never split.

## User Impact

No user-facing behavior change. Element text sent to the LLM context now contains
valid UTF-16 regardless of where emoji fall in the truncated string.

## Evidence

- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `pnpm test extensions/browser/src/browser/cdp.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.snapshot.test.ts` — 2 files, 53 tests passed

## After-fix Proof

Boundary probe for the pattern used across all three paths:

```
input: "x".repeat(99) + "😀" + "rest"  (emoji at position 99–100)

BEFORE .slice(0, 100):
  output: "x".repeat(99) + "\uD83D"   ← lone high surrogate, broken

AFTER [...str].slice(0, 100).join(""):
  output: "x".repeat(99)              ← emoji dropped whole, clean

✅ All three paths produce well-formed UTF-16.
```

## Tests

No new tests added — the fix is a mechanical replacement across three files with
identical semantics (first N code points instead of first N code units). Browser
test suite (53 tests) passes unchanged.
